--- SOURCE 2: Endovascular thrombectomy after 24 hours for patients with acute ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0303846724004979

SUMMARY:
Endovascular thrombectomy after 24 hours for patients with acute ischemic stroke due to large vessel occlusion: A systematic review and meta-analysis of outcomes - ScienceDirect Endovascular thrombectomy after 24 hours for patients with acute ischemic stroke due to large vessel occlusion: A systematic review and meta-analysis of outcomes Another retrospective study demonstrated that EVT 24 hours after symptoms onset in patients with acute ischemic stroke resulting from anterior circulation large-vessel occlusion had good safety and efficacy with better functional independence at 90 days (about 3 months) and no increased risk of intracranial hemorrhage . This study explored the outcomes of patients with acute ischemic stroke due to large vessel occlusion undergoing endovascular therapy (EVT) after 24 hours.

Review article

Endovascular thrombectomy after 24 hours for patients with acute ischemic stroke due to large vessel occlusion: A systematic review and meta-analysis of outcomes
=================================================================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Muhammad Shakir a, Hammad Atif Irshad b, Bilal Ahmed Lodhi b, Zuhaib Ali b, Fizza Zubair b, Muhammad Umar Mahar b, Illiyun Banani b, Amna Wajahat c, Inamullah Khan a, Farhan Siddiq a, Adnan I.Qureshi d

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clineuro.2024.108610](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clineuro.2024.108610 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0303846724004979&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   •The proportion of symptomatic intracerebral hemorrhage (sICH) in patients treated with EVT after 24 hours was 4.74 % (95 % CI: 3.20 %-6.58 %), with no significant difference compared to those treated within 24 hours (RR: 0.85, 95 % CI: 0.44-1.64). 
*   •Functional independence at 90 days (mRS 0-2) was achieved by 35.73 % of patients treated with EVT after 24 hours (95 % CI: 27.26 %-44.64 %), with no significant difference from those treated earlier (RR: 0.85, 95 % CI: 0.34-2.09). 
*   •The 90-day mortality rate in patients treated with EVT after 24 hours was 22.30% (95% CI: 16.12 %-29.09 %), with a risk ratio of 1.80 (95 % CI: 0.73-1.61). 
*   •The overall proportion of intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) following EVT after 24 hours was 12.23 % (95 % CI: 5.47 %-20.86 %). 

Abstract
--------

### Objective

To evaluate the role of endovascular thrombectomy (EVT) in patients presenting with acute ischemic stroke (AIS) due to large vessel occlusion in the very late window (>24 hours).

### Methods

A systematic review was conducted according to PRISMA guidelines using PubMed, CINAHL, Scopus, and Google Scholar databases till 2024. Quality assessment was performed using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS). Outcomes were analyzed with a single-arm meta-analysis (Sidik-Jonkman model) and a double-arm meta-analysis (Mantel-Haenszel model) to compare EVT within and after 24 hours, reporting pooled risk ratios. Analysis was performed using STATA version 18.0 and Review Manager version 5.4.1, with p<0.05 considered significant.

### Results

This review included 35 studies with 15,086 patients. The proportion of symptomatic intracerebral hemorrhage (sICH) in patients treated with EVT after 24 hours was 4.78 % (95 % CI: 3.20 %-6.58 %), with a risk ratio (RR) of 0.85 (95 % CI: 0.44–1.64) compared to EVT patients treated within 24 hours. The pooled percentage for functional independence (90 day mRS 0–2) was 35.73 % (95 % CI- 27.26 %, 44.64 %) with a risk ratio of 0.85 (95 % CI: 0.34, 2.09). The proportion of the 90-day mortality was 22.30 % (95 % CI: 16.12 %, 29.09 %), with a risk ratio of 1.08(95 % CI: 0.73, 1.61). The overall proportion of intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) was 12.23 % (95 % CI: 5.47–20.86) following EVT after 24 hours.

### Conclusion

Patients treated with EVT after 24 hours have comparable safety and effectiveness to those treated within 24 hours. The outcomes suggest that EVT after 24 hours is a viable treatment option, offering similar benefits to earlier intervention.

Introduction
------------

Intravenous thrombolysis (IVT) and endovascular thrombectomy (EVT) are the standard of care for patients with acute ischemic stroke (AIS) due to large vessel occlusion (LVO) [1]. Reperfusion time is an important prognostic factor in acute ischemic strokes. The treatment goal is to achieve timely recanalization of occluded arteries, optimize collateral blood supplies, and avoid secondary brain tissue injury [2]. The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved the use of IVT within 4.5 hours of symptom onset [1], [2]. In 2015, randomized controlled trials (RCTs) demonstrated the effectiveness of EVT performed within an effective window period in the treatment of acute ischemic strokes. Among these RCTs, the MR CLEAN, EXTEND-IA, and SWIFT PRIM trials were conducted within 6 hours of the last known well, the REVESCAT trial was conducted within 8 hours, and the ESCAPE trial was conducted within 12 hours [3].

The last known well (LKW) or last seen well is critical for determining eligibility for interventions. LKW is the time when the patient was last known to be without signs and symptoms of stroke. Before 2018, endovascular treatment was recommended for patients with ischemic stroke due to anterior circulation LVO within 6 hours of the LKW as per stroke guidelines [4]. The DAWN (Diffusion Weighted Imaging or Computerized Tomography Perfusion Assessment with Clinical Mismatch in the Triage of Wake Up and Late Presenting Strokes Undergoing Neuro-intervention) and DEFUSE 3 (Endovascular Therapy Following Imaging Evaluation for Ischemic Stroke 3) clinical trials in 2018 led to new guidelines that recommend EVT within 6–24 hours of LKW for eligible patients with anterior LVO [5]. EVT was also found beneficial in patients with vertebrobasilar strokes up to 24 hours in BAOCHE (Basilar Artery Occlusion Chinese Endovascular) and 12 hours in ATTENTION (Trial of Endovascular Treatment of Acute Basilar-Artery Occlusion) clinical trials [6].

The SELECT Late Study demonstrated that patients who were treated with EVT after 24 hours of the LKW achieved more functional independence and reduced mortality compared to patients who received medical therapy only, which suggests that EVT can improve clinical outcomes in a very late time window (>24 hours) [7]. Another retrospective study demonstrated that EVT 24 hours after symptoms onset in patients with acute ischemic stroke resulting from anterior circulation large-vessel occlusion had good safety and efficacy with better functional independence at 90 days (about 3 months) and no increased risk of intracranial hemorrhage [3]. Similarly, other studies have also found favorable clinical outcomes and good reperfusion rates [8], [9].

Current stroke guidelines [10], [11] do not provide specific indications for managing patients with acute ischemic stroke after 24 hours of LKW. Two reviews have been done previously [6], [12], and despite those, there has been an increase in new literature leading to the need for an updated meta-analysis. Therefore, the objective of this study is to assess and compare the outcomes of EVT in patients with AIS after 24 hours from the LKW.

Section snippets
----------------

Methods
-------

This research is registered on the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (CRD42024529632) and is in adherence with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA).

Results
-------

A total of 1953 studies were initially identified across databases, with 664 duplicates removed before screening. After excluding 1096 titles and abstracts based on eligibility criteria, 35 articles met the criteria for inclusion in the final review, illustrated in Fig. 1. Articles were assessed for risk of bias using NOS. Among them, 25 were rated as good, 2 as fair, and 8 as poor, as detailed in Supplementary Figure 1 and Supplementary Table 1. With the ROB 2.0 tool, the overall assessment of 

Discussion
----------

This study provides insights into the outcomes of EVT beyond 24 hours for acute ischemic stroke due to large vessel occlusion. Our findings suggest that the frequency of symptomatic intracerebral hemorrhage (sICH) and other adverse outcomes, such as mortality and overall intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH), is comparable to that of EVT performed within 24 hours. Furthermore, functional outcomes measured by the 90 day modified rankin scale (mRS) scores indicate no significant difference between EVTs

Strengths
---------

The strengths of this study include the incorporation of recent research up to 2024, improving the relevance and applicability of the findings compared to previous studies. This study also improved statistical rigor by conducting a double-arm meta-analysis, as compared to previous studies.

Limitations
-----------

The analysis was limited by the small number of studies, leading to reduced statistical power, and a decreased ability to detect true effects. This also amplified publication bias, as fewer studies overrepresented favorable outcomes. The limited literature made the findings less generalizable to larger populations and subgroups, complicating the evaluation of heterogeneity and the interpretation of results. A meta-analysis for TICI could also not be conducted due to limited and heterogeneous

Conclusion
----------

This study explored the outcomes of patients with acute ischemic stroke due to large vessel occlusion undergoing endovascular therapy (EVT) after 24 hours. Our findings suggest that the efficacy and safety of EVTs after 24 hours are comparable to those performed within 24 hours of an acute ischemic stroke (AIS) due to large vessel occlusion. Additionally, the study highlighted the significant complications and risks associated with EVT, consistent with evidence from previous research. Further

Disclosures
-----------

The authors have no personal, financial, or institutional interest in any of the drugs, materials, or devices described in this article.

Funding
-------

No funding was received for this study.

Patients with large strokes had a dramatically better recovery after endovascular thrombectomy plus medical management at long-term follow-up.

# International Study Finds Thrombectomy Highly Effective Long-Term Treatment for Large Strokes

February 09, 2024

By
[Ansley Kelm](/news-releases/authors/ansley-kelm)

![Charts from SELECT2 NEJM article](/-/media/images/news/select2-charts.png)

![Charts from SELECT2 NEJM article](/-/media/images/news/select2-charts.png)

**CLEVELAND**—In a major, international study, named [SELECT2](https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2214403), a University Hospitals (UH) research team found that patients with large strokes had a dramatically better recovery after endovascular thrombectomy plus medical management at long-term follow-up, than patients who only received standard medical management.

Endovascular thrombectomy is mechanical recovery of a clot that is blocking blood flow in an artery. Size of a stroke is determined by the range of damaged brain tissue seen through CT or MRI scans. While thrombectomy has proven effective in smaller strokes, patients with large strokes have been considered too high risk for the procedure.

The SELECT2 study was a tremendous, global team effort, spearheaded by UH as the coordinating center. A total of 352 patients, ages 18 to 85, with large ischemic strokes were randomized at 31 hospitals in North America, Europe, Australia and New Zealand. The primary findings of SELECT2 trial were published last year in the [*New England Journal of Medicine*](https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2214403)*,* demonstrating better clinical outcomes in patients receiving thrombectomy at the 90-day follow-up. Those findings, along with other trials, established efficacy and safety of endovascular thrombectomy in patients with large core strokes.

However, long-term outcomes for these patients were not explored before. As these patients have suffered large strokes, it remains plausible that they will take a longer time to achieve optimal stroke outcomes. The SELECT2 research team opted to address this question and analyzed patient outcomes after one year. The study published today in [*The Lancet*](https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(24)00050-3/abstract) to coincide with its presentation at the [International Stroke Conference](https://professional.heart.org/en/meetings/international-stroke-conference) (ISC 2024) in Phoenix.

Patients receiving thrombectomy procedure demonstrated close to three times higher rates of independent living and twice the rates of independent walking. For every six patients treated, one achieved better outcomes as compared to medical management only. For a comparison, balloon angioplasty for heart attack only benefits one in 20-50 patients. Patients also demonstrated better quality of life scores.

Global Principal Investigator and Lead Author of the study, [Amrou Sarraj, MD](https://www.uhhospitals.org/doctors/Sarraj-Amrou-1518264514), said, “Throughout our research and clinical trials over the past three years, we’ve established that endovascular thrombectomy for patients with large ischemic stroke is safe and effective. However, until now, long-term outcomes for these patients remained widely unknown.” Dr. Sarraj is also the Stroke Center and System Director, the George M. Humphrey II Chair in Neurology at University Hospitals, and Professor of Neurology at Case Western Reserve University School of Medicine.

The SELECT2 results represent a major advancement in stroke therapy worldwide, and will bring hope for patients and families in a subgroup of patients who previously would have been thought not to benefit. The study proved that a higher quality of life for these patients persists for at least one year following intervention.

“Now that we have been able to follow these patients for a full year, we have confirmed the long-term benefits of thrombectomy in patients with large core strokes,” Dr. Sarraj explained. “With the evidence that improvement continues, exploration of how we can further improve these outcomes, including efforts on supportive care, such as rehabilitation and adjunctive therapies, becomes very important.”

“University Hospitals is thrilled to have served as the coordinating site for the transformative, international SELECT2 study expanding the indications for thrombectomy in large strokes,” said [Daniel Simon, MD](https://www.uhhospitals.org/about-uh/leadership/uh-system-leadership/daniel-simon), President, Academic & External Affairs, Chief Scientific Officer, and Ernie and Patti Novak Distinguished Chair in Health Care Leadership at University Hospitals. “The results of this study will impact thousands of patients worldwide, significantly improving their quality of life. We look forward to implementing this practice changing treatment for stroke in our health system.” Dr. Simon is also Professor of Medicine and Senior Associate Dean for Academic Affairs at Case Western Reserve School of Medicine.

*The study was funded through a grant from Stryker Neurovascular to University Hospitals.*

*DOI:* [*https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(24)00050-3*](https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(24)00050-3)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Endovascular thrombectomy (EVT) is a minimally invasive procedure that removes blood clots from blocked arteries, significantly improving blood flow in patients with acute ischemic stroke.
Patients undergoing EVT show substantial improvements in functional outcomes, with studies indicating higher rates of independence and better quality of life at long-term follow-ups.

Endovascular thrombectomy has been associated with lower mortality rates in stroke patients, with recent studies showing a 90-day mortality rate significantly reduced compared to standard medical management.
Despite its effectiveness, EVT remains underutilized in clinical practice, often due to delays in diagnosis and treatment initiation, leading to missed opportunities for improved patient outcomes.
Addressing healthcare disparities is crucial, as certain populations may have limited access to EVT, impacting overall treatment equity and outcomes in stroke care.
